sales.title:
CALABRIA. Tarentum. Ca. 302-280 BC. AR didrachm (20mm, 1h). NGC VF. Ar-, Phi-, and Philis- magistrates. Nude warrior on horse rearing right, spearing downwards with right hand, shield and two spears in left; AP monogram behind, ΦI below / TAPAΣ, Taras astride dolphin left, distaff over shoulder in left hand; eagle standing left, with closed wings, behind, ΦIΛIΣ before, waves below. HN Italy 933. Deep charcoal cabinet toning with breathtaking rainbows haloing the devices. Taras, a figure of Greek mythology and the eponymous founder of Tarentum in Calabria, played a pivotal role in the art and culture of the region. Taras was believed to be the son of Poseidon, the Greek god of the sea, and a local nymph named Satyrion. According to mythology, Taras was saved by his father Poseidon when he was thrown into the sea by his mother, who feared his divine nature. He was carried to the shores of southern Italy, where he founded the city of Tarentum. Depicted as a youthful and handsome figure riding a dolphin, Taras symbolized the city's maritime significance and its unique blend of Greek and local influences. His imagery, prevalent in coinage, sculptures, and other artistic representations, served as a source of civic pride and a testament to Tarentum's rich heritage. Taras' influence extended beyond Calabria. The city of Tarentum itself became known for its wealth and cultural achievements, including its distinctive red-figure pottery. The figure of Taras and his association with the sea continued to be influential in the art and culture of the region for centuries. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
Ancients

CALABRIA. Tarentum. Ca. early 3rd century BC. AR stater or didrachm (24mm, 7.73 gm, 6h). NGC Choice XF S 5/5 - 5/5. Arethon, Sa-, and Sas-, magistrates. Nude youth on horse stepping right, crowning horse with right hand, reins in left; ΣA behind, APE/ΘΩN below / TAPAΣ, Taras astride dolphin left, tripod in outstretched right hand; CAΣ below. HN Italy 957. HGC 1, 813. Neon cyan toning bursts out in hand. Taras, a figure of Greek mythology and the eponymous founder of Tarentum in Calabria, played a pivotal role in the art and culture of the region. SICILY. Selinos. AR Didrachm (8.95 gms), ca. 540-515 B.C. NGC MS, Strike: 4/5 Surface: 5/5.
HGC-2, 1210; SNG ANS-681; SNG Cop-593. Obverse: Selinon leaf; Reverse: Incuse square divided into eight sections. A charming high-grade example, well-centered and lacking any weak points in the strike. Rich cabinet tone with hints of amber brilliance around the devices.
From the Peter Jones M.D. Collection (Greek Coin Art pg. 49; #1854).

Ex: Coin Galleries (8/2006) Lot # 148.
To view all items from the Peter Jones M.D. Collection, click here.

sales.title:
MACEDON. Kingdom of Macedon. Archelaos, 413-400/399 B.C. AR Stater (Didrachm) (10.70 gms), Aigai or Pella Mint. NGC EF, Strike: 3/5 Surface: 4/5.
HGC-3.1, 795; SNG ANS-68. Obverse: Head of Apollo right, wearing tainia; Reverse: Horse standing right, with foreleg raised, wearing bridle with trailing rein; all within incuse square. Deeply toned. An attractive example of sound surface quality and an appealing strike.
From the Peter Jones M.D. Collection (Greek Coin Art pg. 57; #2457).

Ex: Davisson's 20 (2/2004) Lot # 29 (with ticket).

sales.title:
MACEDON. Kingdom of Macedon. Philip V, 221-179 B.C. AR Didrachm (8.43 gms), Pella or Amphipolis Mint, ca. 200/197-179 B.C. NGC Ch EF★, Strike: 5/5 Surface: 4/5. Fine Style.
HGC-3.1, 1059; Mamroth-25. Obverse: Diademed head right; Reverse: Club; mintmaster's monogram above, two monograms below; all within oak-wreath; tripod to outer left. A splendid example of the type with a boldly struck portrait in fine style. Both sides are well-centered on the round flan. Very light marks on the otherwise faultless surfaces that flaunt a handsome pale gray tone. An outstanding piece.
From the Peter Jones M.D. Collection (Greek Coin Art pg. 77; #3220).

Ex: Numismatica Ars Classica 138 (5/2023) Lot # 143 (with ticket).
To view all items from the Peter Jones M.D. Collection, click here.
